event: God the Father enthroned among the seraphim
symbols: triple tiara — supreme divine authority, sometimes interpreted as sovereignty over heaven, earth, and the underworld
raised right hand with two fingers — gesture of blessing and Trinitarian affirmation
long flowing beard — ancient of days, eternal wisdom, and patriarchal dignity
red seraphim — burning love and ceaseless worship, the six-winged beings of Isaiah's vision
gold background — the uncreated light of divine glory and heavenly transcendence
elaborate vestments — priestly and royal dignity of the Almighty
description: A half-length depiction of an elderly bearded figure wearing an ornate triple-tiered papal tiara with intricate gold patterning and a halo-like mandorla behind his head. He wears rich red and gold vestments and raises his right hand in a gesture of blessing. His face is lined with age, framed by long wavy gray hair and a full beard. He is surrounded entirely by vivid red six-winged seraphim whose overlapping wings fill the entire background, creating a dense, fiery pattern. The figures are rendered in the International Gothic style with rich gilding and decorative detail.
theological_description: Broederlam's depiction of God the Father from the Altar of Philip the Bold draws directly upon Isaiah's throne-room vision: 'I saw the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up... Above him stood the seraphim. Each had six wings: with two he covered his face, and with two he covered his feet, and with two he flew. And one called to another and said: Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory' (Isaiah 6:1-3). The six-winged red seraphim — their very name meaning 'burning ones' — express the consuming holiness of God that even angelic beings must veil themselves before. The triple crown reflects medieval theological tradition attributing to the Father absolute sovereignty over all realms of existence. The blessing gesture with extended fingers affirms the Father as the source of all benediction, from whom every good gift descends (James 1:17). This image also resonates with the vision of the Ancient of Days in Daniel 7:9, whose 'clothing was white as snow, and the hair of his head like pure wool; his throne was fiery flames.' The fiery red seraphim surrounding the Father embody the declaration that 'our God is a consuming fire' (Hebrews 12:29), while the gold ground represents the unapproachable light in which the divine Majesty dwells.

scripture_refs.reason: The enthroned Lord surrounded by six-winged seraphim crying 'Holy, holy, holy' — the direct visual source for this composition
The Ancient of Days with white hair and fiery throne — the iconographic basis for this elderly, majestic depiction of God
Our God is a consuming fire — embodied by the fiery red seraphim that surround the divine presence
Every good and perfect gift comes from the Father of lights — the blessing hand extends divine benediction
The four living creatures with six wings who never cease saying Holy, holy, holy — the Apocalyptic parallel to Isaiah's seraphim
God dwelling in unapproachable light — the gold ground symbolizing the transcendent radiance of divine glory
